transcript.clean_text: - This bike right here is the brand new Specialized Tarmac. Belongs to Peter Sagan although there's been a few spy shots from races at the Critérium du Dauphiné this is the first time that it's been available to view for the press and for the public. Sagan is gonna choose to ride this bike apparently on all of the hilliest stages and impressively on many of the flat stages. Let's start with the few of the details on the frame. The Specialized Tarmac frameset family has a long and very successful history in road racing. So what do they look to update on this latest version? Well, a few things. First up, light weight. It weighs in at 735 grammes. Sagan's bike is possibly a little bit heavier thanks to the really very cool, custom paint job which adds a bit of weight which means the mechanics don't have to weigh this one down quite as much. Next up: aerodynamics. Specialized have worked very hard to make the Tarmac which is previously known as their lightweight climbing bike into also an aero machine. So this one is more aerodynamic than the first generation Specialized Venge which is of course, Specialized aero bike. Next up is stiffness and ride quality. So this bike is really based on Specialized Rider-First Engineering Philosophy I guess. Which means that the stiffness and ride feel of the bike is appropriate to the size of the rider who might be riding that size of bike. And that starts with frame size and also linked into saddle height. Specialized have also worked on a couple other things: tyre clearance is one because as we know tyres have been getting fatter over the last few years. So this bike has clearance for 30 millimetre tyres. Finally, compliance. So the shape of the seatpost is more compliant as it has a bit more give in it than the traditional round seatposts. And the top section of the seatpost the top 10 centimetres or so are designed to add to that compliance too. Finally the paint job. The mechanics of Bora are actually very relived that Sagan's bike does have such a custom paint job because it adds a little bit of weight which means they have to add less weight to the bike to bring it up to UCI's low weight limit of 6.8 kilos. The paint is what Specialized call their Superstar paint. So it's designed to when the sun's shining it shows all of the colours of the rainbow. Starting as we always like to do at the very top of the bike Sagan is riding a Specialised Romin Evo saddle it's got Fact carbon fibre rails and there are a few custom touches there so the Specialized S is in gold and towards the back there's some colour on it that matches the Superstar paint look of the rest of the bike. Moving forward from that there's a very chunky unbranded 14 centimetre stem which sits on top of about five millimetre of headset spacer which sit in turn, sits on top of a CeramicSpeed headset. The handlebars are Specialized Aerofly they're very similar shape to the aerodynamic handlebars that you might see on the Specialized Venge VIAS that some of the riders on the team use on the flatter days. Attached to this handlebars we've got SupaCaz bar tape Dura-Ace 9150 shifters and a couple of sprint shifters too. Linked up to the brake levers we've got Shimano Dura-Ace 9100 dual mount brakes both front and rear. And we've got Shimano Dura-Ace 9150 Di2 front and rear mech. The rear brake is particularly interesting because instead of having a bridge on the frame Specialized have got what looks like a carbon booster plate there and that is apparently a lighter and slightly stiffer solution for direct mount brakes. Sagan's cassette is Dura-Ace, his chain is Dura-Ace and his chainset is Dura-Ace too. And that has a 4iiii power metre both left and ride sided. Pedals are also Dura-Ace. The wheels are by Roval, a Specialized in-house wheel brand that made the CLX50's which we knew at the start of this year. Tyres are also Specialized and they are the Turbo Elite made with Specialized Gripton rubber. So about the finishing touches on this bike. Well there are a couple of little ones that I really like. Up front, K-Edge have made custom Garmin or custom computer holders for all of the Bora-Hansgrohe riders of this years tour. It's got his nickname on so that's Peto Bomber. And also if we move back to the seatpost the head of the race Specialized 3D printed number holders for all of the riders so that's custom one-off job and they have been epoxied to the seatpost. Vital stats time. Sagan's cranks are 172.5mm His chain rings are 39/53 and his cassette is an 11 through to 28. His stem is 14 centimetres long and his handlebars are 42 centimetres measured centre to centre on the drops. The reach from the nose of his saddle to the centre of the stem comes in at around 60 centimetres and his saddle height from the centre of the bottom bracket to the centre of the saddle comes in around 75 centimetres which is actually just my perfect height. And the way the bike, like we said it is a super light bike that the mechanics are likely to have to weigh down especially if Sagan's teammates whose bikes don't have this sort of paint work on them so it comes in at 6.8 kilos on the dot. I hope you've enjoyed looking at Sagan's bike don't forget to hit the thumbs up button let us know your thoughts down on the comments and share this one with your friends too. To see more pro bikes you better to subscribe to GCN and to do that all you need to do is click or tap on our logo which is on screen right now. Also on screen is a link to our shop. To see more bikes from the Tour de France click right there to check out our full Tour de France playlist and see another randomly selected pro bike just click there.
